curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Understand and apply horizontal translation rules y = f(x + a) and y = f(x - a), explain shift direction using correct transformation language, and connect horizontal shifts to modelling delays and time changes in real-world systems such as transport and scheduling. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

verse_ality.driving_question: How do graphs shift left or right, and how can that help us model time changes and design fairer systems?

knowing_world.focus: Explore horizontal translations by changing the input inside a function, recognising that the shape stays the same while every x-value effectively shifts left or right.
key_concepts: y = f(x + a) shifts left by a units
y = f(x - a) shifts right by a units
activities: Explain why adding inside the function shifts the graph left.
Explain why subtracting inside the function shifts the graph right.

human_story.focus: Horizontal translations model delays, phase shifts, and time changes in life: train schedules, sound engineering, daylight patterns, and accessible transport planning.
justice_prompts: Can you think of something in your world that was delayed — but still arrived?
When we understand how things move in time and space, how can we build smarter, fairer systems for everyone?

expressive_self.focus: Use writing and art to connect horizontal shifts to changes in direction, timing, and personal stories.
activities: Write or sketch a scene where a person’s path changes — just like a graph.
What does it feel like to be “shifted left” or “shifted right”?
Create a metaphor for a graph that slides: “Like time, like tide, like...”

designed_world.focus: Use an interactive Web Sketchpad to test how changing a inside the function shifts the graph and moves key features like the turning point along the x-axis, then connect this to ethical design decisions in scheduling and sensing systems.
tools: Web Sketchpad interactive (embedded via Canvas file link)
activities: Set f(x) = x^2, then adjust values of a to shift it left or right.
Observe how the turning point moves along the x-axis.
design_challenges: Ethical design prompt: Design a schedule or sensor that must shift based on time of day or environment. Explain how horizontal translations help you design for fairness.

regulation_supports: Sit comfortably with both feet on the ground.
Sway slightly to the left, then to the right, noticing balance.
Let your breath follow: in on the left, out on the right.
wellbeing_prompts: Even graphs need room to shift. So do we.

pathways.name: Option 1: Visual Model of a Horizontal Shift
pathways.focus: Create a visual model of a function with a horizontal shift and explain its rule clearly.
products: A labelled graph (paper or digital) showing an original function and a horizontally shifted version
A written or spoken explanation identifying the form and the direction/magnitude of shift
assessment_criteria: Conceptual Understanding – Identifies correct shift direction and form
Fluency & Accuracy – Applies function rules with accuracy
Communication – Clear explanation using correct transformation language

pathways.name: Option 2: Real-Life Application
pathways.focus: Find a real-life application (transport, design, or scheduling) that uses horizontal translation and explain why it matters.
products: A short description of the real-life situation (delay, schedule shift, phase shift, etc.)
A simple function/graph model showing the horizontal translation
A short explanation of what the shift represents
assessment_criteria: Application to Problems – Links graph changes to real-world shifts
Conceptual Understanding – Correctly matches translation to meaning
Independence & Reflection – Thoughtful choice of example

pathways.name: Option 3: Creative Story
pathways.focus: Write a creative short story based on a graph that shifts direction, using the idea of translation as a narrative move.
products: A short story (or comic/voice note) inspired by a graph shifting left/right
A short paragraph or caption linking the story to y = f(x ± a)
assessment_criteria: Independence & Reflection – Shows unique voice and personal meaning
Conceptual Understanding – Correctly links the story to the transformation
Communication – Story is coherent and clearly connected to the maths

required: Plot and interpret points on a coordinate grid
Work confidently with integers (including negative values)
Recognise translations as shifts without changing shape

builds_toward: Full set of graph transformations (translations, reflections, stretches)
Sketching transformed graphs quickly from function notation
Trigonometric phase shifts and modelling periodic behaviour
Function notation fluency for GCSE and beyond
